distribution.iqr_collapse_reason: Q1 = Q3 = 0 because 91.7% of values are zero, so IQR = 0 and the fence collapses — every non-zero value appears as an outlier. This is not a true outlier problem; it is zero inflation.
distribution.recommended_treatment: The 99,999 sentinel value indicates top-coding (values above a threshold were capped at 99,999 in the original census data). Recommended fix: (1) create a binary indicator column `capital_gain_nonzero`, (2) among non-zero values, apply log1p transform to compress the right skew, (3) flag the 159 top-coded rows with a `capital_gain_topcoded` indicator. Do NOT remove rows or apply IQR-based removal — the zeros are structural, not errors.

distribution.iqr_collapse_reason: The IQR is only 5 hours (40–45) because nearly half the dataset works exactly 40 hours/week. This makes the 3x IQR fence very tight (25–60 hours), flagging anyone working part-time or long hours as an outlier. These are real, valid work patterns — not data errors.
distribution.recommended_treatment: Winsorize at the 1st and 99th percentile (roughly 10–80 hours) to preserve the full range of real working patterns while dampening extreme values. Do NOT remove rows — part-time and overtime workers are valid and likely informative for predicting income.

Drop duplicates keeping first occurrence. The Adult Income dataset is known to contain duplicate rows from the original census extraction. These are true duplicates, not legitimate repeated observations.

distribution.mechanism_evidence: Missingness in hours-per-week correlates with workclass (self-employed and without-pay categories have higher missingness rates), suggesting the values are Missing At Random conditional on employment type.
distribution.recommended_treatment: Iterative imputation using correlated columns (workclass, occupation, age) as predictors. KNN is also acceptable. Do not use mean imputation — 14.7% is too high and the narrow distribution around 40hrs means mean imputation would mask meaningful variation.

distribution.mechanism_evidence: Missing workclass likely indicates non-disclosure or non-standard employment status. The fact that it is missing may itself be informative — people who do not report workclass may have different income patterns.
distribution.recommended_treatment: Two-step: (1) create binary indicator column `workclass_was_missing`, (2) fill missing values with the mode ('Private') as a conservative imputation. The indicator column preserves the signal from non-disclosure for the model.
